input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle (Fig. 1) retrofitted as a teleoperated platform with advanced sensor and control technology. It comprises a lower and upper chassis framework that houses the vehicle’s autonomy elements, including the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic. Operating in an on‑road ACC domain, the vehicle supports real‑time monitoring of state, projected path, and detected nearby object tracks. Its compact scale, retrofitted sensor suite, and integrated control stack make it an ideal subject for digital twinning, allowing detailed data collection and remote interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system (DTS) delivers three core services. (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) that visualises real‑time vehicle state, projected path, and detected object tracks while enabling users to view, save, and generate reports of the collected data. (2) Computation Offloading – the DT offloads the ACC processing from the vehicle’s resource‑constrained ECU to remote (cloud/edge) compute, where it determines the lead vehicle, calculates a desired speed based on the distance to the lead vehicle, and returns the speed command to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands via the UI, with the system maintaining low end‑to‑end latency communication to satisfy safety‑critical requirements.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Hardware: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano platform equipped with a powerful GPU for accelerated algorithms and built‑in Wi‑Fi radio for inter‑entity communication. Sensors include high‑definition cameras and LiDAR (advanced sensor and control technology) retrofitted onto the vehicle’s chassis. Software: Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) orchestrates inter‑node communication; high‑level logic is implemented in Python and C++; control models are designed in MATLAB/Simulink; data is persisted in an SQL database for reporting and analytics. The entire DT stack is containerized and orchestrated on the Jetson board, with inter‑entity data transmission occurring over a Wi‑Fi network that satisfies the low latency and reliability required for safety‑critical ACC operations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital twin system requires a shared notion of time across physical and digital components to support computation offloading. Standards mandate clock synchronization tighter than 100 ms for safety‑critical vehicular applications; due to hardware limitations, the design targets a 100 ms absolute time‑accuracy budget. This requirement governs the temporal granularity of service interactions, ensuring that data exchanges and control commands are timestamped with at least 100 ms precision so that physical sensors, actuators, and digital processing elements operate in a temporally coherent manner.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system is structured into three primary entities per the ISO 23247 entity-based model: the User Entity (hosting the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel), the Digital Twin Entity (managing data storage, processing, and control logic), and the Physical Twin Entity (containing resource‑specific functionality such as point‑cloud clustering & tracking, as well as device communication). Each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware component, implying a decentralized deployment of functional fragments. The hierarchical arrangement of entities reflects a three‑layer ownership model, where the Physical Twin handles raw sensor and actuator interfacing, the Digital Twin processes and stores data, and the User Entity exposes visual and control interfaces. No explicit mention of multiple digital twin instances or centralization strategies is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin system follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, mapping software components to Functional Elements (FEs) and implementing each FE as an individual ROS 2 package. These packages encapsulate one or more ROS 2 nodes that provide the intended functionality. The orchestration of the system is thus defined by the entity structure: User Entity (FE split into visualization and control panels), Digital Twin Entity (data management and control mechanisms), and Physical Twin Entity (resource handling and general automotive functions). The design deviates from the reference architecture by introducing the Physical Twin Entity, which consolidates compute, sensor, and actuator resources and supports automotive‑specific operations. Coordination occurs across these entities via ROS 2 messaging, with each FE’s execution context governed by its deployed hardware component. The architecture also includes a Data Storage component that lacks a naturally aligned FE, indicating an explicit allocation decision in the functional mapping.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the vehicle’s LiDAR sensor and vehicle state parameters (e.g., speed, acceleration) are collected by the Physical Twin Entity and transmitted over a Wi‑Fi link as UDP packets to the Digital Twin. Within the Digital Twin the Data Translation component serializes the raw sensor bytes, aligns the message types to the ISO 23247 schema, and performs necessary unit conversions. Internally, the Digital Twin’s software modules communicate via a ROS 2 network, allowing subsystems such as the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component to publish processed point‑cloud data to the 3D Visualization module in real‑time.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands from the Virtual cockpit’s Control Panel (for example: enable/disable Adaptive Cruise Control, modify desired speed, trigger emergency braking) are encapsulated by the Control FE and sent over UDP through the Wi‑Fi link to the vehicle’s Actuation FE. The Actuation FE validates and authenticates each command before forwarding it to the vehicle’s motor, steering, or braking actuators. Feedback from the actuators (e.g., actual speed, braking status) is returned along the same path, enabling the Digital Twin to confirm command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Network infrastructure consists of a dual‑layer architecture: intra‑software communication leverages ROS 2 over a local serial bus or Ethernet, while inter‑entity communication uses Wi‑Fi links carrying UDP packets. The system must sustain a maximum round‑trip latency of 100 ms from the vehicle to the digital twin, as specified by requirement R7, to support timely visualisation and control. Bandwidth requirements are driven by the LiDAR raw point cloud data stream (typically tens of megabytes per second) and vehicle state updates, though explicit limits are not documented. No redundancy or high‑availability topology is specified in the current documentation, nor are specific security protocols (e.g., TLS) described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliant with ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (Part 2 – Reference Architecture), adopting an entity‑based reference architecture comprising Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities within each component may be implemented using OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA). The demonstration is published on a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin) and a YouTube video (https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU), illustrating the interoperability of the reference model with standard OPC UA services.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Compliance with ISO 15622:2018 – The Digital Twin system implements Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) following the performance requirements and test procedures defined in ISO 15622. The system must meet the specified vehicle‑dynamic performance, collision‑avoidance characteristics, and satisfy the integrity tests of this standard, thereby ensuring functional safety of ACC operation. In addition, network requirements are dictated by 3GPP TS 22.185: the Digital Twin must maintain vehicle‑to‑vehicle latency of no more than 100 ms for V2X data exchange, ensuring timely delivery of situational awareness data critical for safe ACC behavior.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It correctly identifies the lower and upper chassis framework, the inclusion of autonomy elements, and the on-road ACC domain. It also mentions real-time monitoring capabilities. However, it includes speculative language like 'ideal subject for digital twinning' which is not explicitly supported by the text, though the suitability is implied by the case study context. The description is specific and technically grounded in the provided text.

The extraction correctly identifies the three core services: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It details specific capabilities such as the UI visualizing vehicle state, projected path, and object tracks; offloading ACC processing to determine lead vehicle and desired speed; and remote management commands like enabling/disabling ACC and emergency braking. It also correctly mentions low end-to-end latency for safety. The description is highly detailed and strongly supported by the source documents.

The extraction correctly identifies specific hardware and software tools present in the text, such as the 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'MATLAB/Simulink', and 'SQL database'. However, the score is reduced because the extraction makes unsupported claims, specifically stating the stack is 'containerized and orchestrated' and describing the GPU as 'powerful', which are not found in the source text.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.' and 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

SW components that implement data management and control mechanisms are organized into the Digital Twin Entity... Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component.'

The description is highly detailed and specific, accurately summarizing the architectural mapping to ISO 23247 FEs, the use of ROS 2 packages, and specific deviations like the Physical Twin Entity and Data Storage mapping. Quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA.', and 'one SW component, Data Storage did not have a naturally aligned FE.'

The characteristic correctly states 'Not in Document'. The provided text focuses on the internal architecture (entities, FEs, ROS 2, Wi-Fi) of the specific Digital Twin System (DTS) and its components, but does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for external connections, or integration with other Digital Twins as defined by the characteristic description.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text details the sensing infrastructure. Section III-B lists specific onboard sensors: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. Figure 2 also depicts the LiDAR component.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source text does not contain specific details regarding model fidelity requirements, validation methods, uncertainty quantification, or error correction for the specific case study implementation. While the introduction generally defines DTs as 'high-fidelity computational reflections' and mentions 'validate compliance' in related work, the specific requirements (R1-R8) focus on latency, clock synchronization, and architecture rather than technical fidelity or validity metrics.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. It is supported by the text: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS 2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U[DP]' and 'This component [Data Translation] serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'. However, the specific mention of 'aligns the message types to the ISO 23247 schema' adds a level of detail not explicitly found in the snippets, though ISO 23247 is the general context.

The extraction correctly identifies the command types (ACC enable/disable, speed modification, emergency braking) and the transmission path via UDP and Wi-Fi to the Actuation FE. This aligns with R5: 'The UI shall implement the following commands: enable and disable ACC; modify the desired speed of ACC; perform emergency braking'. However, the details about 'validates and authenticates each command' and specific feedback content (actual speed, braking status) are not explicitly supported by the provided text snippets.

The description accurately synthesizes the network infrastructure details found in the text. It correctly identifies the dual-layer architecture (ROS 2 internally, Wi-Fi/UDP for inter-entity). It correctly cites the specific latency requirement from R7 (100 ms). It correctly identifies the bandwidth driver (LiDAR) and notes the absence of security or redundancy protocols in the provided text. Quotes supporting this include: 'R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity' and 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text focuses on the system architecture and physical twin hardware but does not contain specific details regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, or scalability mechanisms as defined in the characteristic description. Thus, the claim is justified.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
